notes.anchor: pitched camp at Cybistra, a town under Mount Taurus
notes.note: Cybistra lay in south-eastern Cappadocia at the foot of the Taurus, near the Cilician Gates; Cicero used it as a forward base to watch Syria, Armenia, and the Cappadocian court.
Artavasdes II ruled a kingdom that wavered between Rome and Parthia; Cicero camped near his frontier to remind him a Roman army was at hand.
Ariobarzanes III of Cappadocia; the cult-titles 'Eusebes' (the Pious) and 'Philorhomaeus' (Friend of the Romans) were standard self-styling of the Hellenistic client kings.

notes.anchor: a decree which had never been passed by our order in respect of any king
notes.note: The senatus consultum commending Ariobarzanes's safety to the proconsul was, Cicero stresses, an unprecedented honour for a foreign king; 'our order' is the senatorial order.

notes.anchor: the case of his father's killing
notes.note: Ariobarzanes II of Cappadocia had been murdered (c. 52 BC), so the warning to the young king to guard himself carried recent and personal force.
The younger brother who revealed he had been sounded out to seize the throne but refused while Ariobarzanes lived; he later reigned as Ariarathes X.

notes.anchor: he should use his royal right
notes.note: The ius regium: Cicero advises the king to deal with the conspirators by his own sovereign authority rather than draw on the Roman legions, which were needed at the Cilician frontier.
